meta.descriptionTemplate: Certifications we hold (ISO 9001, Halal) vs. reports provided per order — Certificate of Analysis, third-party lab report, and SHT — on every container.

heroSubtitle: There is an important difference between a certification a factory holds and a report it provides with your shipment. {{brand}} keeps the two separate — held certifications below, per-order reports beneath them — so you know exactly what is verified and by whom.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Request documentation — certifications or a sample COA — by WhatsApp

intro.p1cont: pillar, and it is the one place that draws the line clearly: what the factory is certified for, versus what it documents for each order. Both matter to a wholesale buyer; conflating them is how suppliers oversell.

keyFacts.heading: Documentation at a glance
keyFacts.definition: What {{brand}} holds, what it provides per order, and who verifies it.
keyFacts.reportsValue: Certificate of Analysis + third-party lab report + SHT

held.heading: Certifications & accreditations held
held.intro: These are formal certifications {{brand}} holds. They are verified and third-party — and distinct from the per-order reports further down the page.

iso9001.desc: A quality-management-system certification covering {{brand}}'s production and quality-control processes — the recognized framework for consistent, documented manufacturing quality.

halal.name: Halal certification
halal.desc: Certifies the product as halal — relevant for Saudi Arabia, the wider Gulf, and Muslim-majority markets where halal status is a purchasing requirement.

reports.heading: Test reports provided per order
reports.procedureLinkIntro: Every figure in these reports comes from a defined procedure —
reports.procedureLinkLabel: see the testing methods behind the values
reports.intro: These documents are issued for your specific shipment. They are not certifications the factory holds; they report the measured results for your batch and ship with the cargo.

coa.definition: A Certificate of Analysis (COA) is a document stating the measured specification values for a specific production batch — ash, fixed carbon, moisture, volatile matter, and calorific value — so a buyer can confirm the charcoal meets the agreed specifications for that exact lot.
coa.readLinkIntro: For how to read each value on a COA,

thirdParty.body: An independent laboratory — {{thirdPartyLabsOr}} — tests {{thirdPartyScope}} on {{thirdPartyFrequency}} and issues its own report. Because the lab sits outside the factory, it gives the buyer verification from an independent source, not just the producer's own figures.

sht.body: The Self-Heating Test (SHT) report documents the charcoal's self-heating behavior, tested at an accredited laboratory, and ships as a deliverable with the order.
sht.dgLinkIntro: The dangerous-goods classification this report supports is explained under

items.q: What is a Certificate of Analysis (COA)?
items.a: A Certificate of Analysis (COA) is a document stating the measured specification values for a specific production batch — typically ash, fixed carbon, moisture, volatile matter, and calorific value. It lets a buyer confirm the charcoal meets the agreed specifications for that exact lot. {{brand}} provides a COA with every container, and a sample COA on request.
Yes. {{brand}} holds Halal certification, issued by {{halalBody}}. This matters for Saudi Arabia and the wider Gulf, where halal status is often a purchasing requirement.

items.q: What is a third-party lab report and what does it certify?
items.a: A third-party lab report is testing performed by an independent laboratory — for {{brand}}, {{thirdPartyLabsOr}} — rather than by the factory itself. It reports measured values for {{thirdPartyScope}}, giving the buyer verification from a source outside the producer. It is a per-shipment report, not a certification the factory holds.

Yes. Alongside in-house quality control, an independent third-party laboratory ({{thirdPartyLabsOr}}) tests {{thirdPartyScope}} on {{thirdPartyFrequency}}, and that report accompanies the shipment — so the verification does not rest on the factory's word alone.
